title: Derivative-State Drift
subtitle: A Continuous-Time Model of Constraint Erosion in Elite and Artificial Optimization Systems
title_normalized: derivative-state drift: a continuous-time model of constraint erosion in elite and artificial optimization systems

description: This working paper develops the Derivative-State Drift (DSD) framework as a continuous-time structural model of cumulative misalignment in derivative-based optimization systems. It explains how systems can remain locally coherent and dynamically smooth while gradually diverging from normative reference states when constraints are soft, enforcement is incomplete, and resource buffering attenuates penalties. The framework is applied symmetrically to elite institutional environments and artificial optimization systems, emphasizing architectural isomorphism rather than anthropomorphic analogy.
abstract: This paper develops the Derivative-State Drift (DSD) framework as a general structural account of cumulative misalignment in derivative-based optimization systems. It formalizes agents as operating over continuous-time state vectors and selecting actions according to expected first-order state change rather than absolute state levels. When constraint enforcement is soft, probabilistic, or compensable, sensitivity parameters governing normative boundaries decay endogenously over time. The analysis establishes three core results. First, bounded state velocity does not imply bounded normative deviation. Local dynamical stability is therefore compatible with global misalignment. Second, under incomplete enforcement, constraint sensitivity exhibits monotonic expected decay, deforming the effective constraint manifold without requiring discontinuity in state trajectories. Third, resource buffering attenuates effective penalties and accelerates drift dynamics. The framework is instantiated in two structurally parallel domains: elite institutional environments and artificial optimization systems. Despite differences in substrate, both instantiate derivative-based evaluation under soft constraint regimes and mediated feedback, generating formally equivalent drift dynamics. The comparison is architectural rather than anthropomorphic. The paper concludes that alignment is not primarily a question of intention or performance optimization, but of constraint architecture design. Derivative-State Drift is a general property of optimization systems in which boundaries are compensable and feedback is incomplete. Misalignment thus emerges as the predictable long-run behavior of locally rational systems operating within deformable constraint manifolds.

series_information.series_description: The WP-F series explores AI governance and strategic risk through comparative and historical diagnostics, examining how inherited strategic and regulatory logics break down when transferred into AI-mediated contexts.

methodology.formal_model: Continuous-time derivative-based optimization model with endogenous constraint sensitivity decay
methodology.core_problem: Optimization systems can remain locally stable and performance-improving while drifting away from normative reference states when constraints are soft, compensable, probabilistic, or buffered by resources.
central_claims: Bounded state velocity does not imply bounded normative deviation; local dynamical stability can coexist with global misalignment.
Incomplete enforcement induces monotonic decay of constraint sensitivity and deforms the effective constraint manifold over time.
Resource buffering attenuates effective penalties and accelerates drift by flattening corrective gradients.
Elite institutional environments and AI optimization systems share an architectural isomorphism when both operate through derivative-based evaluation, compensable constraints, and mediated feedback.
Alignment is a problem of constraint architecture design rather than intention or performance optimization alone.

variables.name: Resources
variables.description: Material, informational, computational, or institutional reserves that buffer risk and absorb shocks.

variables.name: Control
variables.description: Capacity to influence institutional processes, decision spaces, or action sets.

variables.name: Access
variables.description: Social or operational legitimacy, including network inclusion, validation, or reputational standing.

variables.name: Constraint
variables.description: Normative alignment parameters, rule adherence, ethical boundaries, or alignment objectives.

main_formulas.name: State vector
main_formulas.description: Continuous-time state vector over resources, control, access, and constraint dimensions.

main_formulas.name: Embedded state transition
main_formulas.description: State transitions are co-determined by agent action and environmental feedback.

main_formulas.name: Derivative-based objective
main_formulas.description: Actions are evaluated according to expected first-order state changes rather than absolute state levels.

main_formulas.name: Action selection rule
main_formulas.description: The agent selects the feasible action maximizing derivative-based utility.

main_formulas.name: Acceptance threshold function
main_formulas.description: Constraint evaluation depends on time-varying sensitivity parameters.

main_formulas.name: Constraint sensitivity drift
main_formulas.description: Sensitivity parameters decay when enforcement is incomplete.

main_formulas.name: Closed-form sensitivity decay
main_formulas.description: Unless enforcement is perfect, parameter erosion is structurally expected.

main_formulas.name: Dynamic interaction between state and threshold
main_formulas.formula: Θ_dot(t) = Σ_i θ_dot_i(t) × S_i(t) + Σ_i θ_i(t) × S_dot_i(t)
main_formulas.description: Threshold change reflects both endogenous erosion of sensitivity and state-driven movement.

main_formulas.name: Derivative localism result
main_formulas.description: Non-negative internal improvement does not imply bounded normative deviation.

main_formulas.name: Effective penalty intensity
main_formulas.description: Resource buffering attenuates the effective force of penalties.

main_formulas.name: Second-order resource amplification
main_formulas.description: Constraint decay accelerates weakly when drift intensity increases with resource buffering.

main_formulas.name: Local dynamical stability condition
main_formulas.description: Local stability describes bounded velocity and smooth trajectory evolution.

main_formulas.name: Global normative stability condition
main_formulas.description: Global normative stability requires bounded deviation from a fixed normative reference state.

theoretical_results.name: Proposition 1: Local Stability Does Not Imply Global Alignment
theoretical_results.summary: Derivative-based systems may exhibit non-negative internal improvement and smooth local dynamics while diverging from normative reference states.

theoretical_results.name: Proposition 2: First-Order Threshold Drift Under Soft Enforcement
theoretical_results.summary: If enforcement is probabilistic with p_i < 1 and penalties do not reset sensitivity parameters, constraint sensitivity decays monotonically.

theoretical_results.name: Proposition 3: Second-Order Resource Amplification of Drift
theoretical_results.summary: Resource buffering attenuates effective penalties and weakly accelerates threshold erosion when drift intensity increases with buffering capacity.

governance_mechanisms: Hard boundaries that function as invariant state constraints rather than compensable penalty terms
Non-transferable enforcement with effective enforcement probability approaching unity
Multi-metric oversight that reduces proxy dominance and distributes derivative incentives
Parameter stabilization mechanisms, including periodic recalibration of constraint weights and independent enforcement layers
Architectural separation between performance maximization and constraint enforcement

dimensions.name: Resources
dimensions.description: Resource buffers that absorb penalties and attenuate corrective force.

dimensions.name: Control
dimensions.description: Capacity to shape decision spaces, institutional processes, or feasible actions.

dimensions.name: Access
dimensions.description: Network legitimacy, validation, reputational standing, or proxy performance access.

dimensions.name: Constraint
dimensions.description: Normative alignment, rule adherence, ethical boundary, or alignment objective dimension.

dimensions.name: Constraint Sensitivity
dimensions.description: Time-varying parameter governing how strongly deviations affect acceptability.

dimensions.name: Enforcement Probability
dimensions.description: Effective probability that a constraint violation triggers correction or penalty.

dimensions.name: Drift Intensity
dimensions.description: Intrinsic or resource-mediated intensity of sensitivity decay.

dimensions.name: Normative Distance
dimensions.description: Distance between the current state trajectory and a fixed normative reference state.

dimensions.name: Local Dynamical Stability
dimensions.description: Bounded instantaneous state velocity and smooth trajectory evolution.

dimensions.name: Global Normative Stability
dimensions.description: Bounded cumulative deviation from the normative reference state.

key_findings: Derivative-State Drift explains cumulative misalignment as endogenous parameter erosion rather than sudden state collapse.
Systems can remain locally coherent, smooth, and performance-improving while diverging globally from normative reference states.
Incomplete enforcement produces first-order monotonic decay of constraint sensitivity parameters.
Resource buffering increases the magnitude and curvature of constraint decay by reducing effective penalty intensity.
Elite institutional environments amplify DSD through high derivative weights on access and control, elastic constraint sensitivity, and resource buffering.
AI optimization systems instantiate DSD through reward gradients, proxy metrics, compensable alignment penalties, and compute surplus.
The comparison between elite systems and AI systems is architectural rather than psychological or anthropomorphic.
Governance should focus on hard boundaries, non-transferable enforcement, multi-metric oversight, and parameter stabilization rather than relying on intention, morality, or performance refinement.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and early ORCID-derived metadata. The working paper presents diagnostic and exploratory analysis intended to examine inherited strategic and governance logics under AI-mediated conditions; it does not constitute policy recommendations, predictive assessments, or official positions of any institution.
